345	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
346	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
347	
348	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
349	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
350	point.
351	
352	\begin{theorem}
353	
354	\leanok
355	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
356	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
357	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
358	  
359	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
360	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
361	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
362	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
363	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
364	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
365	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
366	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
367	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
368	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
369	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
370	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
371	
372	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
373	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
374	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
375	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
376	  in \(X\).
377	\end{theorem}
378	
379	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
380	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
381	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
382	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
383	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
384	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
385	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
386	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
387	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
388	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
389	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
390	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
391	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
392	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
393	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
394	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
395	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
396	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
397	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
398	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
399	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
400	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
401	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
402	% which is a contradiction."
403	\begin{proof}
404	  
405	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
406	
407	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
408	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
409	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
410	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
411	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
412	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
413	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
414	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
415	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
416	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
417	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
418	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
419	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
420	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
421	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
422	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
423	
424	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
425	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
426	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
427	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
428	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
429	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
430	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
431	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
432	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
433	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
434	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
435	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
436	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
437	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
438	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
439	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
440	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
441	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
442	
443	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
444	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
445	  agree on all of \(X\).
446	\end{proof}
447	
448	\begin{remark}
449	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
450	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
451	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
452	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
453	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
454	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
455	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
456	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
457	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
458	
459	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
460	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
461	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
462	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
463	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
464	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
465	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
466	\end{remark}
467	
468	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
469	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
470	
471	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
472	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
473	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
474	this forces the locus to be empty.
475	
476	\begin{lemma}
477	
478	\leanok
479	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
480	  variety]
481	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
482	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
483	  
484	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
485	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
486	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
487	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
488	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
489	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
490	  % divisors).}"
491	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
492	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
493	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
494	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
495	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
496	  union of prime divisors).
497	
498	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
499	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
500	\end{lemma}
501	
502	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
503	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
504	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
505	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
506	% map represented by
507	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
508	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
509	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
510	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
511	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
512	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
513	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
514	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),
515	% \(\varphi\) will be defined on \(U\). For \(u \in U\), the formula
516	%   \(\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)\)
517	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is
518	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
519	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
520	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)
521	% is defined at \((x, x)\) if and only if
522	%   \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}\).
523	% Now \(V \times V\) is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
524	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
525	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),
526	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
527	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
528	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
529	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
530	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
531	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
532	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
533	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
534	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
535	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
536	\begin{proof}
537	  
538	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
539	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
540	
541	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
542	  \[
543	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
544	    \qquad
545	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
546	  \]
547	  via the composition
548	  \[
549	    U \times U
550	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
551	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
552	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
553	  \]
554	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
555	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
556	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
557	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
558	
559	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
560	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
561	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
562	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
563	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
564	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
565	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
566	  identity
567	  \[
568	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
569	    \qquad u \in W',
570	  \]
571	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
572	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
573	
574	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
575	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
576	  \(K(X \times X)\):
577	  \[
578	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
579	  \]
580	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
581	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
582	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
583	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
584	
585	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
586	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
587	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
588	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
589	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
590	  \[
591	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
592	  \]
593	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
594	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
595	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
596	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
597	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
598	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
599	  \[
600	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
601	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
602	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
603	  \]
604	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
605	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
606	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
607	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
608	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
609	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
610	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
611	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
612	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
613	
614	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
615	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
616	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
617	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
618	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
619	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
620	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
621	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
622	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
623	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
624	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
625	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
626	\end{proof}
627	
628	\begin{remark}
629	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
630	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
631	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
632	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
633	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
634	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
635	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
636	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
637	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
638	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
639	\end{remark}

641	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
642	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
643	
644	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
645	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
646	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
647	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
648	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
649	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
650	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
651	
652	\begin{theorem}
653	
654	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
655	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
656	  
657	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
658	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
659	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
660	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
661	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
662	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
663	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
664	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
665	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
666	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
667	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
668	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
669	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
670	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
671	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
672	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
673	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
674	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
675	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
676	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
677	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
678	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
679	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
680	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
681	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
682	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
683	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
684	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
685	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
686	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
687	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
688	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
689	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
690	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
691	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
692	  \begin{enumerate}
693	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
694	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
695	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
696	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
697	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
698	      satisfying
699	      \[
700	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
701	      \]
702	  \end{enumerate}
703	
704	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
705	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
706	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
707	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
708	\end{theorem}
709	
710	\begin{proof}
711	  
712	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
713	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
714	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
715	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
716	  \[
717	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
718	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
719	  \]
720	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
721	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
722	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
723	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
724	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
725	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
726	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
727	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
728	
729	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
730	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
731	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
732	\end{proof}
733	
734	\begin{remark}
735	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
736	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
737	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
738	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
739	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
740	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
741	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
742	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
743	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
744	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
745	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
746	\end{remark}
747	
748	\begin{lemma}
749	
750	\leanok
751	  [Definedness at a prime divisor, repackaged as a through-the-point partial map]
752	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
753	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
754	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
755	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
756	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
757	  following are equivalent:
758	  \begin{enumerate}
759	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
760	      \(W\)).
761	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
762	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
763	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
764	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
765	  \end{enumerate}
766	\end{lemma}
767	
768	\begin{proof}
769	
770	\leanok
771	  The lemma is a definitional reshuffle of the Mathlib membership predicate
772	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain}, which already states that
773	  \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) iff there is some partial-map representative of
774	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
775	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
776	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
777	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
778	  the unfolded definition.
779	\end{proof}

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94

98	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} below
99	% (covering all four conclusions); the three subsequent blocks reference

110	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
111	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
114	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
115	  % extending \(k\)."
116	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
117	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
118	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
119	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
120	  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
121	  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
122	\end{theorem}
123	
124	\begin{theorem}
125	
126	\leanok
127	[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
128	  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
129	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
130	
131	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
132	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
133	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
134	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
135	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
136	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
137	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
138	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
139	  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
140	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
141	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
142	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over
143	  \(\Spec k\) is compatible with the group laws on source and target.
144	\end{theorem}
145	
146	\begin{theorem}
147	
148	\leanok
149	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
150	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
152

154	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at

156	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
157	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
158	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
159	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
160	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
161	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
162	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
163	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
164	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
165	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
166	\end{theorem}

193	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
194	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
195	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
196	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
197	% \(G^0\) is open too.
198	%
199	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
200	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
201	% extending \(k\).
202	%
203	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
204	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
205	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
206	%
207	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
208	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
209	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
210	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
211	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
212	% [\dots]
213	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
214	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
215	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
216	% [\dots]
217	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
218	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
219	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
220	\begin{proof}
221	  We establish
222	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
223	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
224	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
225	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
226	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
227	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
228	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
229	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
230	
231	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
232	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
233	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
234	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
235	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
236	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
237	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
238	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
239	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
240	
241	  \emph{Base-change commutation
242	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
243	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
244	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
245	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
246	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
247	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
248	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
249	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
250	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
251	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
252	
253	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
254	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
255	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
256	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
257	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
258	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
259	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
260	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
261	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
262	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
263	
264	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
265	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
266	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
267	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
268	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
269	  \(\overline k\), the product
270	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
271	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
272	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
273	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
274	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
275	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
276	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
277	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
278	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
279	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
280	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
281	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
282	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
283	\end{proof}
284	
285	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
286	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
287	
288	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
289	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
290	
291	\begin{definition}\leanok
292	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
293	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
295	  
296	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
297	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
298	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
299	  % L2921-L2929).
300	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
301	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
302	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
303	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
304	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
305	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
306	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
307	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
308	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
309	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
310	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
311	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
312	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
313	  % \(k\).
314	  %  \end{prp}"
315	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
316	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
317	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
318	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
319	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
320	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
321	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
322	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
323	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
324	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
325	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
326	  extension of the base field.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\section{The degree map}
330	\label{sec:pic_degree}
331	
332	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
333	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
334	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
335	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
336	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
337	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
338	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
339	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
340	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
341	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
342	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
343	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
344	degree.
345	
346	\begin{definition}\leanok
347	[Degree of a Picard class]
348	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
350	  
351	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
352	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
353	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
355	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
356	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
357	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
358	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
359	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
360	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
361	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
362	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
363	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
364	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
365	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
366	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
367	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
368	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
369	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
370	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
371	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
372	  \[
373	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
374	  \]
375	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
376	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
377	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
378	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
379	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
380	  \[
381	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
382	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
383	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
384	  \]
385	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
386	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
387	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
388	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
389	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
390	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
391	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
392	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
393	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
394	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
395	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
396	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
397	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
398	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
399	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
400	  of degree \(d\).
401	\end{definition}

403	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
404	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
405	
406	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
407	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
408	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
409	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
410	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
411	automatically.
412

422	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. The combined proof,
423	% sharing the \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third
424	% block.

469	[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]

494	\begin{theorem}
495	
496	\leanok
497	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
498	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
500	
501	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
502	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at

508	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
509	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
510	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
511	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)

519	
520	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
521	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
522	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
523	% identity-component substrate.)
524	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
525	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
526	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
527	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
528	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
529	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
530	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
531	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
532	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
533	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
534	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
535	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
536	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
537	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
538	% everywhere."
539	\begin{proof}
540	  We establish
541	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
542	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
543	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
544	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
545	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
546	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
547	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
548	
549	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
550	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
551	  conjuncts from four inputs.
552	
553	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
554	  By
555	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
556	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
557	  type by
558	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
559	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
560	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
561	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
562	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
563	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
564	
565	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
566	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
567	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
568	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
569	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
570	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
571	
572	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
573	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
574	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
575	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
576	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
577	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
578	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
579	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
580	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
581	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
582	
583	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
584	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
585	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
586	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
587	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
588	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
589	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
590	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
591	
592	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
593	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
594	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
595	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
596	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
597	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
598	
599	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
600	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
601	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
602	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
603	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
604	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
605	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
606	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
607	  the open identity component, and
608	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
609	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
610	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
611	
612	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
613	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
614	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
615	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
616	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
617	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
618	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
619	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
620	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
621	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
622	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
623	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
624	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
625	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
626	  zero on \(C\)'').
627	\end{proof}
628	
629	\section{Lean encoding}
630	\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}
631	
632	The Lean target file is
633	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
634	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
635	(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
636	of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
637	
638	\begin{itemize}
639	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
640	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
641	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
642	    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
643	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
644	    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
645	    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
646	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
647	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) packages the
648	    clopen-subscheme inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (open immersion
649	    \emph{and} closed immersion) --- conclusion (a) of Kleiman~\S 5
650	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
651	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
652	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
653	    the group-homomorphism property of the inclusion of
654	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: \(G^0\) inherits a
655	    \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\) compatible with the
656	    inclusion. Conclusion (b) of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
657	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
658	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible})
659	    packages the finite-type-ness and geometric-irreducibility
660	    conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (conclusion (c)).
661	    In Lean these are the instances
662	    \texttt{[LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom]},
663	    \texttt{[QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom]}, and
664	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom]}.
665	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
666	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}) packages the
667	    base-change-commutation conclusion (d) of Kleiman~\S 5
668	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3): for any field extension \(K/k\), the natural
669	    map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) (in Lean,
670	    \texttt{(IdentityComponent G)\_K \(\to\) IdentityComponent (G\_K)})
671	    is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
672	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
673	    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
674	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\) obtained by applying the
675	    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
676	    \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), inheriting the \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
677	    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
678	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
679	    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
680	    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
681	    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
682	    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
683	    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
684	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
685	    component.
686	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
687	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
688	    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
689	    conjunction \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]},
690	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
691	    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
692	    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
693	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
694	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
695	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
696	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
697	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
698	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
699	    underlying scheme).
700	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
701	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
702	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
703	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
704	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
705	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
706	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
707	\end{itemize}
708	
709	The abstract identity-component substrate
710	(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} and its four
711	conclusion-specific theorems
712	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme},
713	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism},
714	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible},
715	\texttt{IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}) is new project material: it
716	is not currently in Mathlib, and the Lean skeleton is owed in a
717	follow-up iteration. The downstream
718	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety},
719	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.finrank\_eq\_genus}, and
720	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.kPoints\_iff\_kerDegree} consume Kleiman~\S 5
721	Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac and Milne~\S III.1, both of which currently
722	live only at the blueprint level; their Lean specialisation to the
723	smooth-proper-curve case is the main substantive step of A.3.
724	
725	\section{Out of scope}
726	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
727	
728	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
729	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
730	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
731	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
732	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
733	
734	\begin{itemize}
735	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
736	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
737	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
738	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
739	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
740	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
741	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
742	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
743	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
744	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
745	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
746	    Route~A.
747	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
748	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
749	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
750	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
751	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
752	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
753	\end{itemize}

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
52	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
53	% the verbatim source-quote in \ is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).
56	
57	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
58	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
59	
60	\begin{definition}\leanok
61	[Hilbert polynomial]
62	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
64	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
65	  % Polynomials" (read from
66	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
68	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
69	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
70	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
71	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
72	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
73	  %  the function
74	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
75	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
76	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
77	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
78	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
79	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
80	  %
81	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
82	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
83	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
84	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
85	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
86	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
87	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
88	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
89	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
90	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
91	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
92	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
93	  III.5.2.}
94	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
95	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
96	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
97	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
98	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
99	  \[
100	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
101	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
102	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
103	  \]
104	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
105	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
106	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
107	  well-defined on connected components.
108	\end{definition}
109	
110	\section{The Quot functor}
111	\label{sec:quot_functor}
112	
113	\begin{definition}\leanok
114	[Quot functor]
115	  \label{def:quot_functor}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
117	  
118	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
119	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
120	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
121	  % + L481-L498).
122	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
123	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
124	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
125	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
126	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
127	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
128	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
129	  %
130	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
131	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
132	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
133	  %
134	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
135	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
136	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
137	  %
138	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
139	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
140	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
141	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
142	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
143	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
144	  %  well-defined, which gives
145	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
146	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
147	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
148	  %  [...]
149	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
150	  %  as a co-product
151	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
152	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
153	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
154	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
155	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
156	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
157	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
158	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
159	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
160	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
161	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
162	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
163	  \[
164	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
165	  \]
166	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
167	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
170	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
171	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
172	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
173	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
174	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
175	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
176	      \(\Phi\).
177	  \end{enumerate}
178	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
179	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
180	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
181	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
182	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
183	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
187	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
188	
189	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
190	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
191	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
192	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
193	
194	\begin{definition}\leanok
195	[Grassmannian scheme]
196	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
198	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
199	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
200	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
201	  % + L773-L850).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
204	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
205	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
206	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
207	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
208	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
209	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
210	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
211	  %  contravariant functor
212	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
213	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
214	  %  set of all equivalence classes
215	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
216	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
217	  %  [...]
218	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
219	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
220	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
221	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
222	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
223	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
224	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
225	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
226	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
227	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
228	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
229	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
230	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
231	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
232	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
233	  \[
234	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
235	    \qquad
236	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
237	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
238	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
239	  \]
240	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely $\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
241	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\( (the Quot functor for \)X = S\(, \)E = V$,
242	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	\begin{theorem}\leanok
246	[Representability of the Grassmannian]
247	  \label{thm:grassmannian_representable}
248	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable}
249	  
250	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
251	  % affine patches" through "Projective embedding"
252	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
253	  % L798-L930).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
255	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), the Grassmannian scheme
256	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
257	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
258	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
259	  %  can be explicitly constructed as follows. [...]
260	  %  the schemes
261	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the
262	  %  \({r\choose d}\) different
263	  %  subsets of  \(\{ 1,\ldots, r\}\) of cardinality \(d\),
264	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle \((\theta_{I,J})\) to form a
265	  %  finite-type scheme \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\). As each \(U^I\) is
266	  %  isomorphic to \(\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}\), it follows that
267	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
268	  %  [...]
269	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
270	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
271	  %  [...]
272	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
273	  %  the determinant line bundle
274	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
275	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
276	  %  [...]
277	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
278	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
279	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
280	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
281	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
282	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
283	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
284	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
285	  Explained Ch.~5).}
286	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
287	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
288	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
289	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
290	  equipped with a tautological quotient
291	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
292	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
293	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
294	  $\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
295	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)$ -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
296	\end{theorem}
297	
298	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
299	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
300	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
301	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
302	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
303	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
304	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
305	\begin{proof}
306	  
307	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
308	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
309	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
310	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
311	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
312	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
313	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
314	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
315	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
316	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
317	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
318	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
319	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
320	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
321	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
322	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
323	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
324	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
325	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
326	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
327	
328	  The universal quotient $u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
329	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\( is given on \)U^I\( by the matrix \)X^I$
330	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
331	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
332	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
333	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
334	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
335	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
336	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
337	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
338	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
339	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
340	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
341	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
342	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism $T \to U^I \subset
343	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\(. Base change from \)\mathbb{Z}$ to a general noetherian
344	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
345	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
346	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
347	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
348	\end{proof}
