23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:cross01_substrate_setup}
25	
26	The Lane B cocycle \texttt{gmScalingP1\_chart\_agreement\_cross01} reduces, after
27	the iter-188 (III.c) separated-locus structural setup, to a single substantive
28	factorisation: a pair morphism
29	\(s_{\text{pair}} : T \to T_0 \times_X T_1\) into a fibre product over the
30	projective line bar \(\mathrm{PLB}_{\overline{k}}\) must factor through the
31	diagonal closed immersion
32	\(\Delta : T_0 \times_{X} T_1 \hookrightarrow T_0 \times_{\overline{k}} T_1\)
33	of the separated-over-\(\overline{k}\) target. Concretely the missing primitive is
34	the equivalence
35	\[
36	  \exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g \iff \mathrm{range}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i)
37	\]
38	for a closed immersion \(i\) with reduced source/target hypothesis, which Mathlib
39	ships only in the kernel-inequality form (\(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\),
40	keyed on \(f.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)). Substrate~1 fills this gap.
41	
42	The same Lane B cocycle, together with the two off-target instances
43	\texttt{gm\_geomIrred} and \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}, also requires
44	\[
45	  \mathrm{IsDomain}\bigl(\mathrm{Away}_{\overline{k}}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\,\overline{k}\bigr)
46	\]
47	for a homogeneous generator \(f\) of positive degree in the
48	\(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading. Substrate~2 supplies this via a
49	canonical iso chain
50	\[
51	  \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \overline{k}[t]
52	  \;\cong\; \mathrm{MvPolynomial}_{\mathrm{Unit}}(\mathrm{Away}\,f)
53	\]
54	followed by localisation at \(X()\), reducing the claim to the
55	already-formalised pattern from \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}.
56	
57	\section{Closed-immersion lift by range inclusion}
58	\label{sec:lift_iff_range_subset}
59	
60	\begin{theorem}[lift through closed immersion via range containment]
61	  \label{thm:IsClosedImmersion_lift_iff_range_subset}
62	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsClosedImmersion.lift_iff_range_subset}
63	  Let \(X,Y,Z\) be schemes (Type \(u\)), let \(i : Z \to X\) be a closed
64	  immersion with \(Z\) reduced, and let \(g : Y \to X\) be a
65	  quasi-compact morphism with \(Y\) reduced. Then there exists a
66	  morphism \(h : Y \to Z\) with \(h \circ i = g\) if and only if the
67	  topological range of \(g\) is contained in the topological range of
68	  \(i\):
69	  \[
70	    (\exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g)
71	    \;\iff\;
72	    \mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}).
73	  \]
74	\end{theorem}
75	\begin{proof}
76	
77	  \emph{Forward direction} is range pushdown: if \(g = h \circ i\)
78	  then every point in \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}})\) is the image
79	  under \(i_{\text{base}}\) of \(h_{\text{base}}(y)\) for the
80	  corresponding preimage \(y\).
81	
82	  \emph{Backward direction} threads the Galois connection between
83	  vanishing-ideal and support of an ideal sheaf. The proof has six
84	  steps; the Mathlib primitives cited are present at the project's
85	  pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}).
86	
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	  \item \emph{Range of a closed immersion is its kernel support.}
89	    Because \(i\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) factors (up to
90	    isomorphism) as the canonical subscheme inclusion of its kernel
91	    ideal sheaf, so
92	    \(\mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}) = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
93	    (Mathlib:
94	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.range\_subschemeι} +
95	    \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toImage}.)
96	  \item \emph{Range of \(g\) lies in \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).}
97	    The unconditional inclusion
98	    \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
99	    is shipped (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.range\_subset\_ker\_support}); under
100	    \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\), \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
101	    equals \(\overline{\mathrm{range}\,g_{\text{base}}}\)
102	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.support\_ker}). Since
103	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\) is closed and contains
104	    \(\mathrm{range}(g)\) by hypothesis, the closure also lies in
105	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\):
106	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support} \subseteq i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
107	    (Mathlib: \texttt{closure\_minimal}.)
108	  \item \emph{\(Y\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(g.\mathrm{ker}\) is radical.}
109	    Under \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\),
110	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{ideal}\,U = \mathrm{ker}(g.\mathrm{app}\,U)\)
111	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.ker\_apply}). Reducedness of \(Y\) makes this
112	    ring kernel a radical ideal pointwise on each affine open, so
113	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical} = g.\mathrm{ker}\).
114	  \item \emph{\(Z\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(i.\mathrm{ker}\) is
115	    radical.} Identical argument with \(i\) in place of \(g\)
116	    (closed immersions are quasi-compact, so the
117	    \texttt{ker\_apply} hypothesis is satisfied automatically).
118	  \item \emph{Galois-connection chain.} Combining Steps~2--4 with
119	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.vanishingIdeal\_support} (which equates
120	    the vanishing ideal of a support with the radical) gives
121	    \[
122	      i.\mathrm{ker}
123	        = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
124	        = \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
125	        \le \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
126	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
127	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.
128	    \]
129	    The middle inequality uses the antitone half of the Galois
130	    connection (\texttt{IdealSheafData.gc}).
131	  \item \emph{Apply Mathlib's kernel-inequality lift.} The proof
132	    concludes by feeding \(\mathrm{hker} : i.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)
133	    to \texttt{IsClosedImmersion.lift} and reading off its
134	    factorisation lemma \texttt{lift\_fac}.
135	  \end{enumerate}
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\section{Tensor reducedness over the algebraic closure}
139	\label{sec:tensor_AwayGm_isDomain}
140	
141	\begin{theorem}[domain structure on \(\mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\)]
142	  \label{thm:gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
144	  Let \(\overline{k}\) be a field, let \(d \ge 1\), and let \(f\) be a
145	  non-zero homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d\) in the
146	  \(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading on
147	  \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\). Then the
148	  tensor product
149	  \[
150	    \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\bigl(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading}\,\overline{k}\bigr)\,f
151	    \;\otimes_{\overline{k}}\;
152	    \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
153	  \]
154	  is a domain (and hence reduced).
155	\end{theorem}
156	\begin{proof}
157	  The proof exhibits a canonical isomorphism of \(\overline{k}\)-algebras
158	  identifying the tensor product with the localisation of a
159	  polynomial ring over a domain at a non-zero-divisor; both operations
160	  preserve the domain property.
161	
162	  \begin{enumerate}
163	  \item \emph{Domain instance on the homogeneous localisation.}
164	    \(\mathrm{Away}\,f := \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading})\,f\)
165	    embeds via the \texttt{val\_injective} bridge into
166	    \(\mathrm{Localization.Away}\,f\); since the polynomial ring
167	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\) is a domain
168	    and \(f \ne 0\), the localisation is a domain, and the bridge
169	    transports the property to \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\). (Already used in
170	    \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}; see
171	    \texttt{GmScaling.lean} L734--738 for the precise instance
172	    threading.)
173	  \item \emph{Collapse the tensor with the polynomial factor.}
174	    Recalling
175	    \(\mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
176	       = \mathrm{Localization.Away}(X()\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k})\),
177	    apply \texttt{MvPolynomial.algebraTensorAlgEquiv} to get
178	    \[
179	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k}
180	      \;\cong\;
181	      \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f),
182	    \]
183	    a \(\overline{k}\)-algebra (in fact \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\)-algebra)
184	    isomorphism.
185	  \item \emph{Push the localisation through the tensor.}
186	    Tensoring with the localisation step
187	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k} \to \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}\)
188	    factors through Mathlib's tensor-base-change lemma
189	    \texttt{IsLocalization.Away.tensorRightEquiv}, yielding an
190	    isomorphism
191	    \[
192	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
193	      \;\cong\;
194	      \mathrm{Localization.Away}(\iota(X())\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f))
195	    \]
196	    where \(\iota\) is the canonical algebra map. The threading uses
197	    \texttt{IsScalarTower} compatibility and the
198	    \texttt{Algebra}/\(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\) instance on the
199	    tensored polynomial ring.
200	  \item \emph{Right-hand side is a domain.}
201	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f)\) is a
202	    polynomial ring over a domain (Step~1), hence a domain by the
203	    standard instance. The generator \(X()\) is non-zero
204	    (\texttt{MvPolynomial.X\_ne\_zero}), so its powers form a
205	    submonoid of the non-zero-divisors
206	    (\texttt{powers\_le\_nonZeroDivisors\_of\_noZeroDivisors}); the
207	    localisation is then a domain by
208	    \texttt{IsLocalization.isDomain\_localization}.
209	  \end{enumerate}
210	
211	  Transferring the domain property along the iso of Step~3 closes the
212	  goal. Reducedness is a direct corollary of being a domain.
213	\end{proof}
214

2	
3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	% --- iter-187 plan-phase additions: pins for the iter-187 refactor's
102	% Step 3 + Step 4 sub-helpers (carrierPresheaf functor + sheaf property)
103	% which are the direct-construction substrate the definition below
104	% consumes (via the iter-186 plan-phase `analogies/ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
105	% Decision 3 + Decision 4 recipe). The Lean declarations are produced

156	\leanok
157	[Carrier-submodule refinement enforcing \(F(\bot)=0\)]
158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
160	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
161	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
166	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
167	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.
168	  The refinement
169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
171	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,
172	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;

176	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).

186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	
189	\leanok
190	[Line bundle of a closed point]
191	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
193	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
204	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
208	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}

269	\begin{definition}
270	
271	\leanok
272	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
273	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
275	  
276	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
277	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
278	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
279	  % of the construction) (read from
280	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
281	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
282	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
283	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
284	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
285	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
286	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
287	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
288	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
289	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
290	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
291	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
292	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
293	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
294	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
295	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
296	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
297	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)
298	  produces a canonical \(\bar k\)-linear map
299	  \[
300	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
301	      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
302	  \]
303	  sending a global section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to the rational
304	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of
305	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into \(\mathcal K_C\) at each point. The map \(\iota\) is
306	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
307	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections
308	  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme \(C\)).
309	
310	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
311	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
312	  takes the same inputs as REF (a curve
313	  \(C\), a point \(P\), and the closedness witness
314	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C.left)}) and produces, for each global
315	  section \(s\) of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) at twist \(0\) in the project's
316	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured global-sections pipeline, the corresponding
317	  element \(\iota(s) \in K(C)\) of the project's function-field carrier
318	  \texttt{C.left.functionField}. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}
319	  and its body is gated on the body of
320	  REF.
321	\end{definition}

323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	\leanok
393	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
394	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
395	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
396	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
397	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
398	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
399	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
400	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
401	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
402	  and only if:
403	
404	  \begin{itemize}
405	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
406	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
407	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
408	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
409	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
410	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
411	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
412	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
413	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
414	  \end{itemize}
415	  In divisorial language (REF,
416	  REF), the two conditions combine to
417	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
418	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
419	  zero global section.
420	
421	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
422	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
423	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
424	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
425	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
426	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
427	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
428	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
429	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
430	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
431	\end{proof}
432	
433	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
434	
435	\begin{lemma}
436	
437	
438	\leanok
439	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
440	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
442	  
443	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
444	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
445	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
446	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
447	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
448	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
449	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
450	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
451	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
452	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
453	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
454	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
455	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
456	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
457	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
458	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
459	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
460	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
461	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
462	  \[
463	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
464	  \]
465	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
466	\end{lemma}
467	
468	\begin{proof}
469	  
470	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
471	  exact sequence
472	  \[
473	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
474	  \]
475	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
476	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
477	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
478	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
479	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
480	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
481	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
482	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
483	  \[
484	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
485	  \]
486	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
487	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
488	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
489	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
490	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
491	  \begin{multline*}
492	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
493	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
494	  \end{multline*}
495	  Two ingredients close the proof:
496	
497	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
498	  hypothesis: by REF,
499	  \[
500	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
501	  \]
502	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
503	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
504	
505	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
506	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
507	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
508	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
509	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
510	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
511	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
512	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
513	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
514	
515	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
516	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
517	  \[
518	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
519	  \]
520	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
521	
522	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
523	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
524	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
525	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
526	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
527	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
528	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
529	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
530	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
531	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
532	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
533	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
534	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
535	  complex degenerates).
536	\end{proof}

538	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
539	
540	\begin{theorem}
541	
542	
543	\leanok
544	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
545	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
549	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
550	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
551	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
552	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
554	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
555	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
556	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
557	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
558	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
559	  % is the Euler characteristic
560	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
561	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
562	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
563	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
564	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
565	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
566	  \[
567	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
568	  \]
569	\end{theorem}
570	
571	\begin{proof}
572	  
573	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
574	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
575	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
576	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
577	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
578	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
579	  \[
580	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
581	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
582	  \]
583	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
584	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
585	  \[
586	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
587	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
588	    \;=\; 2.
589	  \]
590	  By
591	  REF, the second
592	  term vanishes:
593	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
594	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
595	
596	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
597	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
598	  REF computes
599	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
600	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
601	  \[
602	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
603	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
604	  \]
605	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
606	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
607	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
608	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
609	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
610	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
611	  segment yields the short exact sequence
612	  \[
613	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
614	  \]
615	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
616	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
617	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
618	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
619	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
620	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
621	\end{proof}

623	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
624	
625	\begin{corollary}
626	
627	
628	\leanok
629	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
630	single simple pole]
631	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
632	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
633	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
634	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
636	  
637	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
638	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
639	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
640	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
641	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
642	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
643	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
644	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
645	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
646	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
647	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
648	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
649	  \begin{itemize}
650	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
651	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
652	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
653	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
654	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
655	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
656	  \end{itemize}
657	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
658	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
659	\end{corollary}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  By
664	  REF,
665	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
666	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
667	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
668	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
669	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
670	  identification of
671	  REF), is a one-dimensional
672	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
673	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
674	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
675	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
676	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
677	
678	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
679	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
680	  REF the section
681	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
682	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
683	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
684	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
685	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
686	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
687	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
688	  of the statement are now immediate.
689	
690	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
691	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
692	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
693	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
694	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
695	
696	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
697	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
698	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
699	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
700	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
701	  REF's output. The
702	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
703	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
704	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
705	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a

22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
45	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
60	\end{itemize}

38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}

32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:

80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is

48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]

1	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
3	
4	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
7	
8	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
9	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
10	
11	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
12	\[
13	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
14	\]
15	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
16	
17	\begin{definition}\leanok
18	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]
19	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
20	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
21	  
22	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology \(J\), and assume sheafification and \(\Ext\) exist for sheaves of \(k\)-modules on \(J\). For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf functor
23	  \[
24	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
25	  \]
26	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
27	\end{definition}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  
31	  \leanok
32	  The body is a direct categorical composition mirroring the general sheaf-cohomology presheaf functor. Universe pinning is required because the morphism universe of the sheaf category lives one level above the object universe.
33	\end{proof}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Cohomology presheaf, \(H'\) flavor]
37	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf}
39	  
40	  In the setting of Definition~REF, let \(\mathcal F\) be a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf
41	  \[
42	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F) \;:\; C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat
43	  \]
44	  to be the evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
45	\end{definition}
46	
47	\begin{proof}
48	  
49	  \leanok
50	  Direct evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
51	\end{proof}
52	
53	\begin{remark}
54	  For every object \(X\) of \(C\), the underlying type of \(\mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F)(X)\) is definitionally equal to \(H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F)\). The per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is therefore inherited through the \(H'\) wrapper.
55	\end{remark}
56	
57	\begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
58	  The cohomology presheaf functor is declared as a non-computable definition and the presheaf as a non-computable abbrev, matching the reducibility conventions of the general sheaf-cohomology API. The abbrev form is load-bearing: downstream definitional unfolding needs to see through to the fiber-level \(H'\) carrier.
59	\end{remark}

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined
11	% that Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) ships every
12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}

20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46

59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
